expanded_description: A clear introduction to plant hormones for secondary school biology. This covers the five major plant hormones—auxins, gibberellins, cytokinins, abscisic acid, and ethylene—how they are made, how they work, what they do, and how farmers and gardeners use them to grow better plants.

core_concept.principle: Plant hormones are chemical messengers that plants make in tiny amounts to control their growth, development, and responses to the environment. Each hormone has specific jobs, but they often work together—sometimes helping each other, sometimes opposing each other.
core_concept.explanation:
Plants cannot move. If a plant is in the shade, it cannot walk to the sun. If a plant is attacked by insects, it cannot run away. Instead, plants use chemical signals—hormones—to change how they grow and respond to their environment.

Plant hormones are different from animal hormones. Animals have glands (like the thyroid or pituitary) that make hormones. Plants make hormones in many different tissues—shoot tips, root tips, young leaves, developing seeds. And plant hormones work at very low concentrations—parts per billion or even parts per trillion.

There are five classical plant hormones. Auxins promote cell elongation, keep the main stem dominant (apical dominance), and make roots grow. Gibberellins make stems grow tall, break seed dormancy, and help fruits grow. Cytokinins promote cell division, delay aging (senescence), and work with auxins to control root vs. shoot growth. Abscisic acid (ABA) is the stress hormone—it closes stomata during drought and keeps seeds dormant. Ethylene is a gas that makes fruits ripen, leaves fall, and flowers fade.

Hormones do not work alone. They interact. Auxin and cytokinin work against each other: high auxin makes roots; high cytokinin makes shoots. Gibberellin and ABA work against each other: GA promotes germination; ABA keeps seeds dormant. Ethylene and auxin sometimes work together—auxin can make ethylene, and ethylene can affect auxin transport.

Hormones are also used in agriculture. Gardeners use auxin rooting powder to make cuttings grow roots. Farmers spray gibberellin on grapes to make them larger. Ethylene is used to ripen bananas after shipping. Cytokinins keep cut flowers fresh longer. And ABA is being studied to help crops survive drought.

Understanding plant hormones helps us understand how plants grow, how to grow better crops, and how to keep flowers fresh longer. It also shows us how plants—even though they cannot move—are constantly sensing and responding to their environment.

image.alt: A diagram of a plant showing where each hormone is made and what it does: auxin (shoot tip, cell elongation), gibberellins (young leaves, stem growth), cytokinins (root tips, cell division), ABA (stressed leaves, stomatal closure), ethylene (ripening fruit, senescence)
image.caption: Plant hormones: Auxin (shoot tip—elongation, dominance), Gibberellins (young leaves—stem growth), Cytokinins (root tips—cell division), ABA (stressed leaves—stomatal closure), Ethylene (ripening fruit—ripening, senescence).

sections.introduction:
Imagine you are a plant. You cannot move. You cannot run from a hungry caterpillar. You cannot walk to a sunnier spot. You cannot tell your roots to grow deeper when the soil gets dry. How do you survive?

Plants use chemical signals—hormones—to coordinate their growth and respond to their environment. These signals travel through the plant (or just act locally), telling cells to divide, elongate, or change what they are doing. Hormones allow a plant to bend toward light, to drop its leaves in autumn, to ripen its fruit when seeds are ready, and to close its stomata when water is scarce.

The study of plant hormones began with Charles Darwin. He noticed that grass seedlings bent toward light. He wondered how they sensed light and how they bent. He did experiments showing that the tip of the seedling senses light, but the bending happens lower down. Something was traveling from the tip to the bending zone. That "something" turned out to be auxin—the first plant hormone discovered.

Since Darwin's time, scientists have discovered five major plant hormones (and several more). Each has its own job, but they all work together in a network. Some hormones help each other (synergy); some oppose each other (antagonism). This network allows the plant to fine-tune its growth to match its environment.

Plant hormones are used widely in agriculture and gardening. Rooting powder contains auxin to help cuttings grow roots. Gibberellin sprays make grapes larger. Ethylene gas ripens bananas after they are shipped. Cytokinins keep cut flowers fresh. And scientists are working on ways to use ABA to help crops survive drought.

Understanding plant hormones helps us understand how plants work—and how we can work with plants to grow more food, prettier flowers, and healthier gardens.

introduction_image.alt: A timeline of plant hormone discoveries: Darwin's phototropism experiments (1880), Went's auxin discovery (1926), gibberellins (1930s), cytokinins (1950s), ABA (1960s), ethylene (1960s)
introduction_image.caption: Hormone discovery timeline: Darwin (1880, phototropism) → Went (1926, auxin) → Gibberellins (1930s) → Cytokinins (1950s) → ABA (1960s) → Ethylene (1960s). Each discovery revealed how plants control growth.

key_concept.definition: Plant hormones are chemical messengers made in tiny amounts that regulate plant growth, development, and stress responses. Each hormone has specific effects, but they interact to fine-tune plant behavior.
key_concept.context:
Hormones work at very low concentrations—often parts per billion. A single hormone can have different effects depending on where it acts and how much is present. The same hormone that makes roots grow at low concentration can stop root growth at high concentration.

Hormones are made in specific tissues. Auxin is made in shoot tips and young leaves. Cytokinins are made in root tips. Gibberellins are made in young leaves and developing seeds. ABA is made in stressed leaves and roots. Ethylene is made in ripening fruit and aging leaves.

Hormones move through the plant in different ways. Auxin moves directionally from shoot tip to root tip (polar transport). Gibberellins and cytokinins move in the xylem and phloem. ABA moves in both xylem and phloem. Ethylene is a gas—it diffuses through the air.

Hormones often work together. The ratio of auxin to cytokinin tells a plant whether to grow roots or shoots. The ratio of gibberellin to ABA tells a seed whether to germinate or stay dormant. This network allows the plant to integrate multiple signals and respond appropriately.

image.alt: A diagram showing that hormones work at low concentrations, have multiple effects, and interact with each other
image.caption: Hormone characteristics: Work at low concentrations (10⁻⁹ to 10⁻⁶ M), have multiple effects (same hormone does different things in different places), interact (synergy or antagonism).

sections.key_concept: The five major plant hormones—auxins, gibberellins, cytokinins, abscisic acid, and ethylene—each have distinct roles, but they work together in a network to control plant growth, development, and stress responses.

classifications.value: Understanding auxins explains how plants grow toward light, why the main stem grows taller than branches, and how to make cuttings root.
characteristics: Main auxin: Indole-3-acetic acid (IAA). Made from the amino acid tryptophan.
Made in: Shoot tips, young leaves, developing seeds.
Transport: Moves directionally from shoot tip to root tip (polar transport).
Effects: Cell elongation, apical dominance (main stem suppresses branches), root formation, fruit development, bending toward light (phototropism).
Agricultural uses: Rooting powder (NAA, IBA), herbicides (2,4-D kills broadleaf weeds), fruit set promotion.
classifications.behavior:
Auxin makes cells elongate. It does this by making the cell wall more acidic, which loosens it so water pressure can expand the cell. This is called acid growth.

Auxin is also responsible for phototropism—plants bending toward light. When light hits one side of a shoot tip, auxin moves to the shaded side. The cells on the shaded side elongate more, bending the plant toward the light. The same principle works for gravitropism—roots growing downward, shoots growing upward.

Auxin causes apical dominance—the main stem grows taller than the branches. The shoot tip makes auxin, which travels down the stem and suppresses the growth of side buds (axillary buds). If you cut off the shoot tip (pruning), the auxin source is removed, and side buds grow. This is why pruning makes plants bushy.

Auxin also promotes root formation. This is why rooting powder (which contains synthetic auxins like NAA or IBA) helps cuttings grow roots. It is also why auxin is used to make fruit set—spraying auxin on tomato flowers can make them set fruit even without pollination.

examples.explanation:
Place a potted plant near a window. In a few days, it will be leaning toward the light. How does it know? The answer is auxin.

The shoot tip senses light (blue light). When light comes from one side, auxin moves to the shaded side of the tip. Then, auxin travels down the stem. On the shaded side, auxin makes cells elongate. On the lighted side, cells do not elongate as much. The stem bends toward the light.

This response helps the plant reach more light. It is why seedlings in a dark room will bend toward a tiny crack of light. It is why houseplants lean toward windows. It is also why plants in the shade grow taller—they are trying to reach the light.

Scientists have studied phototropism for over 100 years. Darwin's experiments showed that the tip senses light, and something travels down to cause bending. That "something" turned out to be auxin.

image.alt: A diagram showing light coming from one side, auxin moving to the shaded side, and cells elongating to bend the plant toward the light
image.caption: Phototropism: Light from one side → auxin moves to shaded side → cells on shaded side elongate more → plant bends toward light. Auxin distribution controls bending.

examples.explanation:
Look at a pine tree. It has one main trunk with branches coming off. This is apical dominance—the main stem (apex) dominates over the side branches. The shoot tip makes auxin, which travels down and suppresses the growth of side buds (axillary buds).

If you cut off the main shoot tip (pruning), the auxin source is gone. The side buds are released from suppression and start growing. The plant becomes bushier.

Gardeners use this all the time. Pinch back a tomato seedling, and it will grow more branches and produce more fruit. Prune a rose bush, and it will produce more flowers. Top a chrysanthemum, and it will grow fuller.

Different plants have different levels of apical dominance. Pines have strong apical dominance—they grow tall and straight. Bushes have weak apical dominance—they naturally branch. By pruning, we can make even strong apical dominance plants become bushier.

image.alt: A diagram showing an intact plant (shoot tip makes auxin, side buds suppressed) and a pruned plant (auxin source removed, side buds grow)
image.caption: Apical dominance: Shoot tip auxin → suppresses side buds. Pruning removes auxin source → side buds grow → bushier plant. Used in gardening to shape plants.

classifications.value: Understanding gibberellins explains why some plants grow tall, why seeds germinate, and how to make grapes larger.
characteristics: Over 130 gibberellins (GAs) exist; GA₃ is most common in research and agriculture.
Made in: Young leaves, developing seeds, root tips.
Transport: Moves in xylem and phloem (no polar transport).
Effects: Stem elongation, seed germination, flowering, fruit growth.
Agricultural uses: Increase grape size, promote barley germination (malt production), make plants grow taller.
classifications.behavior:
Gibberellins make stems grow longer. They cause internodes (the spaces between leaves) to elongate. This is why some plants are tall and others are short. The Green Revolution wheat varieties were short because they had less gibberellin or were less sensitive to it. Short stems are good for grain crops—they don't fall over (lodge) when heavy with grain.

Gibberellins also break seed dormancy. Seeds that need light to germinate often need gibberellin to start growing. In barley seeds, gibberellin tells the aleurone layer (the outer layer of the endosperm) to make enzymes that break down starch into sugar. This sugar feeds the growing embryo. This is how malt is made for beer—barley is soaked, it starts to germinate, and gibberellin is produced.

Gibberellins also make fruits grow. Seedless grapes are treated with gibberellin to make them larger. Without seeds, they don't make enough of their own gibberellin. Spraying them with GA₃ makes them grow to normal size.

Gibberellins work by removing a brake on growth. The plant has proteins called DELLAs that stop growth. Gibberellin tells the cell to destroy DELLAs. With the brake removed, growth can happen.

examples.explanation:
Plants have a built-in brake on growth. This brake is a protein called DELLA. When DELLA is present, growth is slow. When DELLA is destroyed, growth speeds up.

Gibberellin is the key that removes the brake. When gibberellin is present, it binds to a receptor, and together they tag DELLA for destruction. The cell destroys DELLA, and growth genes are turned on.

This is why gibberellin makes plants grow tall. It removes the brake.

The Green Revolution wheat varieties have a DELLA protein that cannot be destroyed. Even when gibberellin is present, the brake stays on. The plants are short. Short wheat is good because it doesn't fall over when heavy with grain. This allowed farmers to use more fertilizer without the wheat lodging, and yields doubled.

image.alt: A diagram showing DELLA protein blocking growth, and gibberellin causing DELLA destruction to allow growth
image.caption: DELLA brake: DELLA protein blocks growth. Gibberellin binds receptor → tags DELLA for destruction → growth proceeds. Green Revolution wheat has DELLA that cannot be destroyed → short, sturdy stems.

examples.explanation:
A seed is a dormant embryo surrounded by food. It will not germinate until conditions are right. Gibberellin is one of the signals that tells the seed to start growing.

In a barley seed, the embryo produces gibberellin when it senses water and warmth. The gibberellin travels to the aleurone layer (the outer layer of the endosperm). There, it triggers the production of enzymes that break down starch into sugar. The sugar feeds the growing embryo.

This is how malt is made. Barley is soaked (to start germination) and then dried (to stop it). The gibberellin has already done its work—the starch has been converted to sugar. The malt is then used to make beer.

Other seeds need other signals to germinate. Some need light (which triggers gibberellin production). Some need cold (which breaks down ABA). Some need fire (which breaks the seed coat). But in many seeds, gibberellin is the final common pathway—it tells the seed to start growing.

image.alt: A diagram of a barley seed showing gibberellin from embryo stimulating aleurone to make enzymes that break down starch
image.caption: Gibberellin and germination: Embryo makes GA → GA signals aleurone → aleurone makes enzymes → starch broken to sugar → sugar feeds growing embryo. Basis for malting (beer production).

classifications.value: Understanding cytokinins explains how plants grow new shoots, why leaves stay green, and how to keep cut flowers fresh.
characteristics: Main cytokinin: Zeatin. Found in many plants.
Made in: Root tips, developing seeds, fruits.
Transport: Moves in xylem (from roots to shoots).
Effects: Cell division, shoot formation, delay leaf aging (senescence), nutrient mobilization.
Agricultural uses: Tissue culture (shoot induction), delay leaf yellowing, keep cut flowers fresh.
classifications.behavior:
Cytokinins make cells divide. They work with auxin to control whether a plant grows roots or shoots. In tissue culture (growing plants from small pieces), high cytokinin and low auxin make shoots; low cytokinin and high auxin make roots; balanced makes callus (undifferentiated cells). This is how scientists clone plants.

Cytokinins also delay aging (senescence). When a leaf is old, it turns yellow as chlorophyll breaks down. Cytokinins can slow this down. This is why cut flowers stay fresh longer if you add cytokinin to the water. It is also why the "Richmond-Lang effect"—a piece of leaf with cytokinin stays green while the rest yellows.

Cytokinins are made in the roots and travel up to the shoots. This allows the roots to tell the shoots about soil conditions. If the soil is dry, roots make less cytokinin, and shoot growth slows. If the soil is wet, roots make more cytokinin, and shoots grow faster. This is one way plants coordinate root and shoot growth.

Cytokinins also work against auxin in apical dominance. Auxin from the shoot tip suppresses side buds; cytokinins from the roots promote side bud growth. The balance between them determines how bushy a plant is.

examples.explanation:
Take a leaf. Cut it in half. Put one half in water with cytokinin, the other in plain water. The half with cytokinin stays green for days or weeks. The other half yellows and dies.

This is the Richmond-Lang effect. Cytokinins delay leaf aging (senescence). They keep chlorophyll from breaking down. They keep proteins from being degraded. They keep the leaf green and functional.

Gardeners use this. Cut flowers placed in water with a little cytokinin will last longer. Florists use preservatives that contain cytokinins (among other things) to keep flowers fresh.

Plants use cytokinins naturally. When a leaf is young, it makes its own cytokinins. As it ages, it makes less. Eventually, the leaf stops making them, and senescence begins. The leaf yellows and falls. This is why leaves fall in autumn—as days shorten, cytokinin production drops, and the leaves senesce.

image.alt: A leaf half treated with cytokinin (stays green) and half untreated (yellows)
image.caption: Richmond-Lang effect: Cytokinin-treated leaf stays green; untreated leaf yellows. Cytokinins delay aging (senescence). Used to keep cut flowers fresh longer.

examples.explanation:
In tissue culture, scientists grow whole plants from tiny pieces of tissue (explants). They put the explant on a gel with nutrients and hormones. By changing the ratio of auxin to cytokinin, they can make the explant grow roots, shoots, or undifferentiated cells (callus).

High auxin/low cytokinin → roots. Low auxin/high cytokinin → shoots. Balanced → callus.

This is how plants are cloned. Take a piece of a desirable plant (like a prize orchid). Put it on medium with high cytokinin. Shoots form. Move shoots to medium with high auxin. Roots form. Plant in soil. Now you have many identical copies of the original plant.

This is used for commercial propagation of orchids, bananas, and other crops. It is also used to produce virus-free plants (the meristem is often virus-free, and can be grown into a whole plant).

image.alt: A diagram showing explant on medium with different auxin/cytokinin ratios producing roots, shoots, or callus
image.caption: Tissue culture: High auxin/low cytokinin → roots. Low auxin/high cytokinin → shoots. Balanced → callus. Enables cloning of orchids, bananas, and other plants.

classifications.value: Understanding ABA explains how plants survive drought, why seeds stay dormant, and how plants prepare for winter.
characteristics: Single major form: ABA (abscisic acid). Made from carotenoids (pigments).
Made in: Stressed leaves (drought), roots (dry soil), developing seeds.
Transport: Moves in xylem (from roots to leaves) and phloem.
Effects: Stomatal closure, seed dormancy, growth inhibition, stress gene induction.
Agricultural uses: Antitranspirant (reduce water loss), dormancy induction, stress tolerance enhancement.
classifications.behavior:
ABA is the stress hormone. When a plant is short of water, ABA closes the stomata. This reduces water loss. ABA also slows growth—no point in growing when water is scarce. And ABA turns on stress genes that help the plant survive.

ABA is made in the roots when the soil dries. It travels up the xylem to the leaves. There, it binds to receptors in guard cells. This triggers a chain of events that makes the guard cells lose water and close. The stomata close within minutes of the roots sensing dry soil—before the leaves themselves get dry. This is an early warning system.

ABA also keeps seeds dormant. A seed that germinates too early (while still on the mother plant) would die. ABA prevents this. It builds up in the seed as it matures, keeping it dormant. To germinate, the seed must break down ABA (or become less sensitive to it). This is why some seeds need to be chilled (winter) before they will germinate—cold breaks down ABA.

ABA also helps plants prepare for winter. It induces bud dormancy, so trees don't try to grow in the middle of winter. It also helps plants survive freezing by turning on cold-tolerance genes.

examples.explanation:
Imagine a hot, dry day. The soil is drying out. The roots sense this and start making ABA. The ABA travels up the xylem to the leaves. There, it reaches the guard cells.

ABA binds to receptors on the guard cells. This triggers a cascade: calcium ions rush in, potassium ions rush out, water follows. The guard cells lose turgor (become floppy) and the stomata close.

Within minutes of the roots sensing dry soil, the stomata are closed. Water loss is reduced. The plant saves water.

This is why plants wilt on hot, dry days. They are not just losing water—they are actively closing their stomata to save water. The wilting is a side effect of the guard cells losing turgor.

When it rains, the roots stop making ABA, and existing ABA is broken down. The guard cells take up potassium again, the stomata open, and the plant resumes photosynthesis.

image.alt: A diagram showing ABA from roots traveling to guard cells, causing K⁺ efflux, water loss, and stomatal closure
image.caption: ABA action: Roots sense dry soil → make ABA → ABA travels to leaves → guard cells release K⁺ and water → stomata close → water loss reduced. Rapid response (minutes).

examples.explanation:
A seed is a baby plant waiting for the right conditions to grow. If it germinates too early—while still on the mother plant, or during a warm spell in winter—it will die. ABA keeps it from germinating too soon.

As a seed matures, ABA builds up. It turns on genes that keep the seed dormant. It also turns off genes that would make it germinate. The seed is now ready to wait.

To germinate, the seed must break dormancy. This can happen in several ways. Some seeds need light—light triggers the breakdown of ABA. Some seeds need cold (stratification)—cold temperatures slowly break down ABA. Some seeds need to be scratched (scarification)—this lets water in, which washes out ABA. Some seeds just need time—after-ripening gradually reduces ABA sensitivity.

When ABA levels drop, gibberellin levels rise. The GA/ABA ratio shifts. The seed germinates. This is why seeds know when to germinate: they sense the right conditions (light, temperature, moisture) and adjust their hormone balance accordingly.

image.alt: A diagram showing ABA maintaining seed dormancy, and conditions (light, cold) reducing ABA to allow germination
image.caption: ABA and dormancy: ABA keeps seeds dormant. Light, cold, or time reduce ABA → GA rises → GA/ABA ratio shifts → germination. Seeds germinate only when conditions are right.

classifications.value: Understanding ethylene explains why fruits ripen, why leaves fall, and why plants get shorter when they hit an obstacle.
characteristics: Ethylene (C₂H₄) is a gas. The simplest alkene.
Made in: Ripening fruits, senescing leaves, stressed tissues.
Transport: Diffuses through air spaces; affects nearby plants.
Effects: Fruit ripening, leaf and flower senescence, abscission (leaf drop), triple response (seedling response to obstacles).
Agricultural uses: Fruit ripening (bananas, tomatoes), flower senescence delay, abscission control.
classifications.behavior:
Ethylene is a gas. It is the only gaseous plant hormone. It diffuses through the air and can affect neighboring plants. This is why a ripe apple can make other apples ripen—it releases ethylene.

Ethylene makes fruits ripen. In climacteric fruits (apple, banana, tomato), a burst of ethylene production triggers ripening. The fruit softens, changes color, becomes sweet, and develops aroma. In non-climacteric fruits (citrus, grapes), ethylene does not have this effect.

Ethylene also makes leaves and flowers age. When a flower is pollinated, it produces ethylene, which causes the petals to wilt and fall. This is why cut flowers last longer if you keep them away from ripening fruit (which releases ethylene). Florists use ethylene inhibitors (like silver thiosulfate) to keep flowers fresh.

Ethylene causes leaves to fall (abscission). In autumn, as days shorten, leaves stop making auxin, and ethylene increases. The leaf makes a layer of weak cells at its base (abscission zone), and the leaf falls.

Ethylene also causes the triple response in seedlings. When a seedling hits an obstacle (like a rock), it produces ethylene. The stem stops elongating, gets thicker, and grows horizontally to go around the obstacle. This helps the seedling avoid damage.

examples.explanation:
A green tomato is hard, sour, and not very tasty. Then, something happens. It turns red, softens, and becomes sweet. That something is ethylene.

As the tomato matures, it starts making ethylene. The ethylene triggers a cascade of changes: cell wall enzymes soften the fruit, pigments change from green to red, starches turn to sugars, and aroma compounds are made. The fruit is now ripe.

Farmers use this knowledge. Bananas are picked green and shipped. When they arrive at their destination, they are exposed to ethylene gas. They ripen quickly and evenly. This allows bananas to be shipped long distances without spoiling.

You can do this at home. Put a green banana in a paper bag with a ripe apple. The apple releases ethylene, and the banana will ripen faster.

Ethylene inhibitors (like 1-MCP) are used to delay ripening. Apples stored in 1-MCP stay crisp for months. This allows apples to be sold year-round.

image.alt: A diagram showing a green banana exposed to ethylene turning yellow, and a diagram of the ethylene biosynthesis pathway
image.caption: Ethylene and ripening: Ethylene triggers fruit softening, color change, sugar production. Used commercially: bananas picked green, ripened with ethylene at destination. 1-MCP blocks ethylene, delays ripening.

examples.explanation:
A seedling growing in the dark is trying to reach the light. But what if it hits a rock? If it kept growing straight, it would damage itself. Instead, it changes its growth pattern. This is the triple response.

When a seedling's tip hits an obstacle, it produces ethylene. The ethylene causes three changes. First, the stem stops elongating (so it doesn't push harder into the obstacle). Second, the stem gets thicker (so it can push around the obstacle). Third, the stem grows horizontally (so it goes around the obstacle).

This response is so specific that it is used to study ethylene. Mutants that do not respond to ethylene (etr1) do not show the triple response. Mutants that make too much ethylene (ctr1) show it all the time, even without an obstacle.

The triple response is a good example of how plants sense their environment and change their growth to avoid damage. It is also a reminder that plants are not passive—they are constantly sensing and responding.

image.alt: A diagram showing normal seedling (long, thin, straight) and ethylene-treated seedling (short, thick, horizontal)
image.caption: Triple response: Ethylene → stem stops elongating, gets thicker, grows horizontally. Helps seedlings go around obstacles. Mutants with no ethylene response (etr1) don't show it; mutants with too much (ctr1) show it always.

sections.section_title: Fundamental Principles of Plant Hormone Action

principles.statement: The same hormone can have different effects depending on how much is present. Low concentrations may stimulate; high concentrations may inhibit.
principles.application:
Auxin is a good example. At low concentrations (10⁻⁹ M), auxin promotes root growth. At higher concentrations (10⁻⁵ M), it inhibits root growth but promotes shoot growth. This is why the same hormone can do different things in different parts of the plant.

This is also why herbicides like 2,4-D work. 2,4-D is a synthetic auxin. At high concentrations, it causes uncontrolled growth in broadleaf plants, killing them. Grasses are less sensitive, so they survive. This is how selective herbicides work.

Plants use concentration gradients to pattern their growth. In the root tip, auxin is most concentrated at the very tip, where it promotes root growth. Farther back, auxin concentration is lower, and it promotes different processes. The gradient tells cells where they are in the root.

This principle applies to other hormones too. Gibberellin at low concentrations promotes stem elongation; at high concentrations, it may cause abnormal growth. Ethylene at low concentrations promotes ripening; at high concentrations, it causes senescence.
principles.example: Root growth vs. shoot growth: A root tip has high auxin concentration. This promotes root growth. A shoot tip also has high auxin concentration, but it promotes shoot growth. The same hormone, different effects, because the tissues are different. This is why we say hormones have "multiple effects"—they do different things in different places.
principles.analogy: Hormone concentration is like the volume on a stereo. Low volume (low concentration) might be soothing (stimulation). Medium volume might be optimal. High volume (high concentration) might be painful (inhibition). The same music (hormone) can have different effects depending on how loud it is.

image.alt: A graph showing root growth and shoot growth at different auxin concentrations
image.caption: Concentration effects: Low auxin (10⁻⁹ M) promotes root growth; high auxin (10⁻⁵ M) inhibits root growth but promotes shoot growth. Same hormone, different effects at different concentrations.

principles.principle: Hormones Work Together in a Network
principles.statement: Hormones rarely work alone. They interact—some help each other (synergy), some oppose each other (antagonism). This network allows fine-tuning of plant responses.
principles.application:
The auxin-cytokinin ratio controls root vs. shoot development. High auxin/low cytokinin = roots. Low auxin/high cytokinin = shoots. This is used in tissue culture to make plants from explants.

The GA-ABA ratio controls seed germination. High GA/low ABA = germination. Low GA/high ABA = dormancy. Seeds integrate environmental signals (light, temperature) to shift this balance.

Ethylene and auxin interact. Auxin can induce ethylene production, and ethylene can affect auxin transport. This is how they work together to cause fruit ripening and leaf abscission.

This network allows the plant to integrate multiple signals. For example, a seed might get light (promotes GA), warm temperature (promotes GA), and moisture (reduces ABA). It integrates all these signals and decides to germinate. If any signal is missing (cold, dark, dry), it stays dormant.
principles.example: Apical dominance involves multiple hormones. Auxin from the shoot tip suppresses side buds. Cytokinins from the roots promote side buds. Ethylene may also be involved. The balance between these hormones determines how bushy a plant is. This is why pruning (removing auxin source) makes plants bushier—it shifts the balance toward cytokinins.
principles.analogy: Hormone interactions are like the controls on a car. The gas pedal (auxin) makes you go faster. The brake (cytokinin) slows you down. The steering wheel (ethylene) changes direction. You need all of them to drive smoothly. Plants use multiple hormones to control their growth.

image.alt: A diagram showing auxin and cytokinin antagonism (root vs. shoot), GA and ABA antagonism (germination vs. dormancy), auxin and ethylene synergy
image.caption: Hormone interactions: Auxin/cytokinin (antagonistic, root vs. shoot). GA/ABA (antagonistic, germination vs. dormancy). Auxin/ethylene (synergistic, some effects). Network allows fine-tuning.

principles.principle: Hormones Help Plants Respond to Their Environment
principles.statement: Plants use hormones to sense and respond to light, water, temperature, and other environmental cues. This allows them to grow in the best possible way for their conditions.
principles.application:
When a plant is in shade, it produces more auxin and gibberellin to grow taller and reach the light. This is shade avoidance. When it is in full sun, it makes less of these hormones and grows more compactly.

When the soil is dry, roots produce ABA, which travels to the leaves and closes the stomata. This reduces water loss. The plant also makes less gibberellin, so it grows slower. This conserves water.

When days get short in autumn, leaves produce less auxin and more ethylene. The leaves form an abscission zone and fall off. The plant also makes more ABA, which puts buds into dormancy for the winter.

When a fruit is ripe, it produces ethylene, which triggers ripening. This ensures that the fruit is ready to eat when the seeds are ready to be dispersed.
principles.example: When a plant is shaded, the ratio of red to far-red light changes. The plant senses this through a protein called phytochrome. This triggers increased auxin and gibberellin production. The plant grows taller (to reach the light), has fewer branches (to focus on upward growth), and flowers earlier (to reproduce before being overtopped). This is the shade avoidance syndrome.
principles.analogy: Hormones are like the plant's control panel. Light sensor (phytochrome) tells the panel "it's sunny" or "it's shady." Water sensor (roots) tells it "soil is wet" or "soil is dry." The panel (hormone network) adjusts the growth accordingly. This is how plants optimize their growth for their environment.

image.alt: A diagram showing light (phytochrome) affecting auxin and gibberellin, water (roots) affecting ABA, temperature affecting ABA and gibberellin
image.caption: Environment → hormones: Light (auxin, GA), Water (ABA), Temperature (ABA, GA). Hormones translate environmental signals into growth responses. Plants optimize growth for conditions.

components.definition: Hormone action involves three steps: synthesis (making the hormone), transport (moving it to where it is needed), and reception (cells sensing it and responding). Each step can be regulated.
components.interpretation: How a plant responds to a hormone depends on how much hormone is present (concentration), how sensitive the cells are (how many receptors they have), and what other signals are present (context). This allows the same hormone to have different effects in different situations.

image.alt: A diagram showing synthesis (where made), transport (how it moves), reception (receptors on target cells), and response
image.caption: Hormone action: Synthesis → Transport → Reception → Response. Each step can be regulated. Concentration, sensitivity, and context determine the outcome.

categories.description: Auxin moves directionally from shoot tip to root tip. This creates gradients that pattern plant growth.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Auxin moves in a special way. Unlike other hormones that move in the xylem and phloem, auxin moves cell to cell in a directional manner. This is called polar transport.

How does it work? Cells have special proteins that pump auxin in (AUX1/LAX) and special proteins that pump it out (PIN). The PIN proteins are located on one side of the cell. In the shoot, they are at the bottom of each cell, so auxin moves down. In the root, they are at the top of each cell, so auxin moves up toward the root tip.

This polar transport creates gradients. In the shoot tip, auxin is high; in the root tip, auxin is also high (because it accumulates there). These gradients tell cells where they are in the plant and what to become.

Polar transport is also responsible for tropisms. When light hits one side of a shoot, PIN proteins move to the opposite side, and auxin flows toward the shaded side. This creates a gradient that causes bending.

examples.explanation:
PIN proteins are like one-way valves. They pump auxin out of cells. Where they are located determines the direction of auxin flow.

In the shoot, PIN proteins are at the bottom of cells. So auxin flows downward toward the root.

In the root, PIN proteins are at the top of cells (toward the shoot). So auxin flows upward toward the root tip? Wait, that would send it back up. Actually, in the root, auxin flows from the base to the tip, then back up the other side. The PIN proteins are arranged to create a circulation pattern.

This circulation creates an auxin maximum at the root tip. This maximum tells the root tip to keep growing. Without it, the root would stop.

When a plant is bent over, PIN proteins redistribute. Auxin flows to the lower side, causing bending. This is how gravitropism works.

image.alt: A diagram showing PIN proteins at the bottom of shoot cells (auxin moves down) and at the top of root cells (auxin circulates)
image.caption: Polar transport: PIN proteins (pumps) at specific cell sides direct auxin flow. In shoots: auxin moves down. In roots: auxin circulates, creating a maximum at the tip. PIN redistribution causes tropisms.

categories.type: Hormone Receptors: How Cells Sense Hormones
categories.description: Cells have special proteins (receptors) that bind hormones. When a hormone binds, it triggers a chain of events inside the cell that leads to a response.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Different hormones have different receptors. Auxin receptors are inside the cell (TIR1). Gibberellin receptors are also inside (GID1). ABA receptors (PYR/PYL) are also inside. Ethylene receptors (ETR1) are on the membrane. Cytokinin receptors (AHK) are on the membrane.

When a hormone binds to its receptor, it causes a change. For auxin, gibberellin, and ABA, the change leads to the destruction of a repressor protein. The repressor was blocking growth genes; when it is destroyed, growth happens. For ethylene, the opposite happens: the receptor normally blocks the response; when ethylene binds, the block is removed.

This is why hormones work at such low concentrations. One hormone molecule can trigger the destruction of many repressors, which can turn on many genes. This is signal amplification.

examples.process: How Auxin Works: Destroying the Brake
examples.explanation:
Imagine a cell that wants to grow. There is a brake (Aux/IAA protein) that is blocking the growth genes. The cell cannot grow until the brake is removed.

Auxin comes in. It binds to a receptor (TIR1). The receptor now grabs the brake and tags it for destruction. The cell destroys the brake. The growth genes are now free to be turned on. The cell grows.

This is how auxin works. It doesn't turn on growth directly. It removes something that was blocking growth. This is called "relief of repression."

This is also why auxin works so fast. The brake is destroyed within minutes. The cell can respond quickly to changes in auxin concentration.

image.alt: A diagram showing auxin binding TIR1, which tags Aux/IAA (brake) for destruction, releasing ARF (growth promoter)
image.caption: Auxin mechanism: Auxin binds TIR1 receptor → TIR1 tags Aux/IAA (brake) for destruction → Aux/IAA destroyed → growth genes activated → cell grows. Relief of repression.

worked_examples.title: Understanding Hormone Control of Branching
worked_examples.problem: You have a plant with strong apical dominance (single main stem, few branches). You do several experiments: (a) Remove the shoot tip → axillary buds grow. (b) Apply auxin to the cut stump → buds remain suppressed. (c) Apply cytokinin to axillary buds → buds grow even with shoot tip present. (d) Measure auxin in stem (high), cytokinin in roots (high), ethylene in nodes (detectable). Based on this, (1) Which hormones promote branching? Which inhibit? (2) How do they interact? (3) How would you make this plant bushier?

step_by_step.explanation: Identify hormones that promote or inhibit branching.
step_by_step.calculation: Auxin inhibits branching (removing tip releases buds; applying auxin restores suppression). Cytokinins promote branching (applying to buds makes them grow). Ethylene may also inhibit branching (present in nodes, where auxin acts).

Auxin from shoot tip travels down, suppressing buds directly. Auxin also induces ethylene in nodes, which also suppresses buds. Cytokinins from roots promote buds, counteracting auxin. Balance determines branching: high auxin/ethylene, low cytokinin → few branches; low auxin, high cytokinin → many branches.

step_by_step.explanation: Propose how to make the plant bushier.
step_by_step.calculation: To make bushier: (1) Remove shoot tip (pruning) → reduces auxin. (2) Apply cytokinin to buds → promotes growth. (3) Apply ethylene inhibitor → reduces suppression. Gardeners use pruning to make plants bushier. Cytokinin sprays are used on some ornamentals to promote branching.

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Inhibit: auxin, ethylene. Promote: cytokinins. (2) Interactions: auxin and ethylene work together to suppress buds; cytokinins counteract them. (3) To make bushier: prune (remove auxin source), apply cytokinins, use ethylene inhibitors. This shows how multiple hormones control plant architecture.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Apical dominance is controlled by a balance of hormones: auxin and ethylene inhibit branching; cytokinins promote branching. By manipulating this balance (pruning, applying hormones), we can control plant shape.

sections.introduction: Hormone signaling follows a sequence: hormone is made, transported to target cells, binds receptors, triggers a cascade, and causes a response. Understanding this flow reveals how plants turn chemical signals into growth changes.

process_flow.description: Hormones are made in specific tissues and transported to where they are needed.
process_flow.mechanism: Auxin: made in shoot tip, young leaves; moves down via polar transport. Cytokinins: made in root tips; move up in xylem. Gibberellins: made in young leaves, seeds; move in phloem/xylem. ABA: made in stressed roots/leaves; moves in xylem/phloem. Ethylene: made in ripening fruit, aging leaves; diffuses as gas.
process_flow.result: Hormone reaches target tissue at appropriate concentration.

image.alt: A diagram showing auxin moving down (polar transport), cytokinins moving up (xylem), ABA moving in xylem and phloem
image.caption: Hormone transport: Auxin (polar, down), Cytokinins (xylem, up), Gibberellins (phloem/xylem), ABA (xylem/phloem), Ethylene (diffusion). Different transport matches different functions.

process_flow.description: Hormone binds receptor, triggering a chain of events inside the cell.
process_flow.mechanism: Auxin (TIR1) → tags repressor for destruction. Gibberellin (GID1) → tags DELLA (brake) for destruction. ABA (PYR/PYL) → inhibits phosphatase, activates kinases → ion channels open. Ethylene (ETR1) → inactivates receptor, removes block. Cytokinin (AHK) → phosphorylation cascade to nucleus.
process_flow.result: Signal amplified; transcription factors activated or released.

image.alt: A diagram showing auxin (destroys repressor), GA (destroys DELLA), ABA (activates kinases), ethylene (inactivates receptor)
image.caption: Signal transduction: Auxin/GA destroy repressors (relief of repression). ABA activates kinases. Ethylene inactivates receptor. All lead to gene expression changes.

process_flow.description: Activated transcription factors turn on or off genes, leading to a physiological response.
process_flow.mechanism: Transcription factors bind DNA → genes turned on/off. Examples: Cell wall enzymes (auxin), α-amylase (GA), stress proteins (ABA), ripening enzymes (ethylene). Response can be growth, division, ripening, or stress tolerance.
process_flow.result: Physiological change appropriate to the signal.

image.alt: A diagram showing transcription factors binding DNA and turning on genes that lead to growth, ripening, or stress responses
image.caption: Gene expression: Transcription factors activate/repress genes → cell wall enzymes (auxin), α-amylase (GA), ripening enzymes (ethylene), stress proteins (ABA) → physiological response.

sections.section_title: Advanced Concepts: Hormone Engineering and Agriculture
sections.definition: Understanding plant hormones allows us to manipulate plant growth for agriculture, horticulture, and forestry. Hormone-based products and genetic modifications improve yield, stress tolerance, and quality.

process_types.type: Green Revolution: Gibberellin and Semi-Dwarf Wheat
process_types.energy_profile: Semi-dwarf wheat puts more energy into grain and less into stem, increasing yield.
process_types.direction: Traditional: Tall stem → lodging (falling over) → reduced yield. Semi-dwarf: Short stem → no lodging → increased yield.
process_types.function: Reduce stem growth to prevent lodging, allowing higher fertilizer use and higher yield.
goals: Respond to fertilizer without falling over
process_types.energy_source: Same photosynthesis; energy reallocated from stem to grain.

primary_example.process: Rht Genes and the Green Revolution
primary_example.explanation:
In the 1960s, Norman Borlaug and his team developed semi-dwarf wheat varieties that changed agriculture. Traditional wheat had tall stems. When farmers added fertilizer, the plants grew taller, and the heavy grain heads made them fall over (lodge). Lodged wheat is hard to harvest and yields less.

The new varieties had short, sturdy stems. They could support heavy grain heads without falling. Farmers could use more fertilizer, and the wheat would not lodge. The result was doubled yields.

The semi-dwarf trait came from genes called Rht (reduced height). These genes make a DELLA protein that cannot be destroyed by gibberellin. The growth brake stays on, so the stem stays short. But the grain heads are still large.

This is a classic example of hormone manipulation. By changing how the plant responds to gibberellin, breeders created plants that put more energy into grain and less into stem. The Green Revolution saved millions from starvation.

image.alt: A comparison of traditional wheat (tall, lodged) and semi-dwarf wheat (short, standing)
image.caption: Green Revolution: Traditional wheat (tall, lodges) → Semi-dwarf wheat (short, stands). Rht genes cause DELLA protein that cannot be destroyed by GA → short stem. Higher yield, less lodging.

image.alt: Applications of hormone manipulation in agriculture: rooting powder (auxin), fruit size (GA), ripening (ethylene), stress tolerance (ABA)
image.caption: Hormone applications: Auxin (rooting cuttings, herbicides), Gibberellin (fruit size, malt production), Cytokinin (delay senescence), Ethylene (ripening), ABA (drought tolerance).

process_types.energy_profile: Controlling ethylene allows fruits to be shipped long distances and stored for months.
process_types.direction: Ethylene present → rapid ripening → spoilage. Ethylene blocked → delayed ripening → longer storage.
process_types.function: Delay ripening during transport and storage; induce ripening when desired.
goals: Induce uniform ripening at destination (ethylene gas)
Extend shelf life of cut flowers
process_types.energy_source: Ethylene produced by fruits; external ethylene applied for ripening.

primary_example.process: 1-MCP: Blocking Ethylene to Keep Fruit Fresh
primary_example.explanation:
Apples, bananas, and other fruits ripen in response to ethylene. Once they start ripening, they produce more ethylene, causing a burst of ripening. This is good when you want to eat them, but bad when you want to store them.

1-MCP (1-methylcyclopropene) is a chemical that blocks ethylene receptors. When apples are treated with 1-MCP, they do not respond to ethylene. They stay crisp and fresh for months in cold storage. When they are removed from storage, they ripen normally.

This technology has revolutionized the fruit industry. Apples can be stored for up to a year and sold fresh year-round. Bananas are picked green, shipped, then ripened with ethylene at the destination.

1-MCP is also used for cut flowers. Flowers treated with 1-MCP last longer because they do not respond to the ethylene they produce as they age.

image.alt: A diagram showing ethylene causing ripening, and 1-MCP blocking the receptor to delay ripening
image.caption: 1-MCP: Blocks ethylene receptors → fruit does not ripen → longer storage. When removed, fruit ripens normally. Used for apples, bananas, cut flowers.

image.alt: Postharvest ethylene management: bananas ripened with ethylene at destination, apples stored with 1-MCP
image.caption: Postharvest management: Ethylene gas ripens bananas at destination; 1-MCP blocks ethylene, allowing long-term apple storage. Cut flowers treated with 1-MCP last longer.

sections.relationship: Green Revolution and postharvest management are two examples of using hormone knowledge to improve agriculture. One focuses on growth (GA), the other on ripening (ethylene). Both show how understanding plant hormones can feed the world.

sections.purpose: These are the essential vocabulary terms for understanding plant hormones. Knowing these terms will help you describe how plants control their growth and respond to their environment.

activation.definition: The terms below cover the most important concepts in plant hormone biology. Each term is defined simply, with context to help you remember its significance.
activation.effect: Mastering these terms will allow you to discuss how plants grow, how they respond to stress, and how we use hormones in agriculture.

image.alt: A labeled diagram of a plant with hormone names and effects: auxin (shoot tip, elongation), gibberellin (young leaves, stem growth), cytokinin (root tips, cell division), ABA (stressed leaves, stomatal closure), ethylene (fruit, ripening)
image.caption: Key hormone terms: Knowing where each hormone is made and what it does helps you understand plant growth and responses.

key_properties.explanation: Hormone that promotes cell elongation, apical dominance, and root formation. Made in shoot tips and young leaves. Moves directionally (polar transport). Used in rooting powder and herbicides.
Hormone that promotes stem elongation, seed germination, and fruit growth. Made in young leaves and developing seeds. Over 130 forms exist. Used to make grapes larger and to produce malt.
Hormone that promotes cell division, shoot formation, and delays aging. Made in root tips. Moves up in xylem. Used in tissue culture and to keep cut flowers fresh.
Stress hormone that closes stomata during drought and keeps seeds dormant. Made in stressed leaves and roots. Helps plants survive drought and cold.
Gaseous hormone that promotes fruit ripening, leaf and flower senescence, and abscission (leaf drop). Made in ripening fruits and aging tissues. Used to ripen bananas and other fruits.
Suppression of side buds (axillary buds) by the shoot tip. Caused by auxin from the shoot tip. Pruning removes the tip, releasing side buds to grow.
Growth toward light. Caused by auxin moving to the shaded side of the shoot tip, causing cells there to elongate more. Bends the plant toward the light.
Growth in response to gravity. Shoots grow up (negative gravitropism); roots grow down (positive gravitropism). Caused by auxin redistribution.
Directional movement of auxin from shoot tip to root tip. Occurs cell to cell via PIN proteins. Creates auxin gradients that pattern growth.
Growth repressors that block growth genes. Gibberellin triggers their destruction, allowing growth. Green Revolution wheat has DELLA that cannot be destroyed, so it stays short.
Repressor proteins that block auxin response genes. Auxin triggers their destruction, allowing auxin response genes to be turned on.
Seedling response to ethylene: stem stops elongating, gets thicker, and grows horizontally. Helps seedlings go around obstacles.
Fruits that ripen in response to a burst of ethylene (e.g., apples, bananas, tomatoes). Non-climacteric fruits (citrus, grapes) do not have this burst.
Chemical that blocks ethylene receptors. Used to delay ripening of apples and other fruits during storage, and to keep cut flowers fresh longer.
Reduced height genes in wheat. Cause a DELLA protein that cannot be destroyed by gibberellin, resulting in short, sturdy stems. Key to the Green Revolution.
Aging of plant tissues. Cytokinins delay senescence; ethylene promotes it. Leaves yellow and fall as part of senescence.

summary:
Plant hormones are chemical messengers that control growth, development, and stress responses. The five major hormones are auxins, gibberellins, cytokinins, abscisic acid (ABA), and ethylene.

Auxins promote cell elongation, apical dominance, and root formation. They move directionally from shoot to root (polar transport). They are responsible for phototropism (bending toward light) and gravitropism (roots down, shoots up). Gardeners use auxin rooting powder to make cuttings grow roots. Synthetic auxins like 2,4-D are used as herbicides.

Gibberellins promote stem elongation, seed germination, and fruit growth. They work by destroying DELLA proteins that block growth. The Green Revolution wheat varieties have DELLA proteins that cannot be destroyed, so they stay short—this prevents lodging and increases yield. Gibberellin is used to make grapes larger and to produce malt for beer.

Cytokinins promote cell division and shoot formation, and delay aging (senescence). They are made in root tips and move up in the xylem. In tissue culture, high cytokinin makes shoots; high auxin makes roots. Cytokinins keep cut flowers fresh longer.

Abscisic acid (ABA) is the stress hormone. It closes stomata during drought, keeps seeds dormant, and helps plants survive cold. ABA is made in stressed roots and leaves. When soil dries, roots make ABA, which travels to leaves and closes stomata within minutes.

Ethylene is a gas that promotes fruit ripening, leaf and flower senescence, and leaf drop (abscission). It causes the triple response in seedlings—short, thick, horizontal growth that helps them go around obstacles. Farmers use ethylene to ripen bananas after shipping; 1-MCP blocks ethylene to keep apples fresh for months.

Hormones work together in a network. Auxin and cytokinin balance determines root vs. shoot growth. GA and ABA balance controls seed germination. Ethylene and auxin interact to cause fruit ripening and leaf drop.

Understanding plant hormones helps us grow better crops, keep flowers fresh, and store fruit longer. It also reveals how plants—even though they cannot move—constantly sense and respond to their environment.
